sentences.text: Yes.
I was in-- in-- oh, I should say we-- were in a terrible mental stage, too.
Because, first of all, we felt we were in-- we saved ourselves.
And we left-- in my case, I left my mother.
I left all my friends.
I left small children still in Italy.
I had to believe that when Dr.
Lichtheim told me the money went to the frontier and went into Chichibo's hands, I had to believe that Chichibo would be-- Chichibo would be able to go back.
But all those things, sitting in-- in Busserach, I didn't know.
I learn from that later on when my mother came and Joschko and the children came.
But in-- in those days of uncertainty, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was under terrible mental pressure, not only me, our little group.
Now, the Swiss in Busserach, that was a military-run camp, extremely strict.
The food supply was completely limited to a quarter-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quarter-pound triangle of cheese and potatoes boiled in water.
I'll tell you.
At the time I crossed the border, the Italian military [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] disintegrated.
They didn't want to fight the war anymore.
And hundreds of soldiers, from officer rank down to the lowest soldier, tried to escape Italy and the-- and the possibility that they had to fight under German command still in the war.
So they-- they escaped to-- to Switzerland.
And the tragic part of the whole is that the Swiss accepted them more readily than the Jewish refugees, just because they had a uniform on.
In our camp in Busserach, most [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people were actually ex-Italian soldiers, coming at the same time I came over the border, perhaps in different locations, into Switzerland.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] To-- to come back to Busserach-- Busserach, the camp, was an old factory.
They made bricks there before they converted it to an camp.
They-- the war was just-- the war, I said.
The walls were just brick walls.
Water was coming down the brick wall.
In the beginning, we had snow on the floor to sleep on.
That was never changed.
And they had an idea that in the morning, we were driven out of the factory into the open, and we had to stay in the courtyard on our feet for hours.
And that was a very cold winter, too, October '43.
We were underfed, underclothed.
And only occasionally, by the time of meals, were we allowed back into the house, into the-- into the-- into the building.
So Busserach was a-- a bad time.
The Swiss segregated man and woman.
The-- the woman that-- Elise Asher is her name-- was interned in an woman camp.
Offhand, I don't know the-- the name.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To come back now to my mother-- I shouldn't say my mother first-- to the-- to the children's group, including my mother, miraculous.
Chichibo, Pacifici, made his way back to Nonantola.
At that time, all the more adult children hiding out by the farmers couldn't stay there anymore.
They made their way south to the-- to the frontier lines between Italy, or German troops now, and American and British forces fighting, or they joined the partisans, like Max Federman did.
Chichibo contacted Joschko, and they decided to take the whole group that was now close to 100 children and eight adults.
And I want to tell you that all the Italian adults left.
They went back home.
So only Mr.
Avien, ?] the cook, my mother, Joschko, you know, a few left, and only Chichibo stayed with us.
They had an very emotional good-bye with the nuns.
And they made their way, miraculously, the same way, with open freight cars and the-- the most horrifying situations to Modena, and from Modena to Milano, from Milano to Como, [?
Chiasso ?] to [?
Varese. ?] I think it took them two days.
And in [?
Varese, ?] Joschko asked Chichibo to come with us, to come with him to Switzerland.
And Chichibo refused.
And Chichibo said, there are more Jews left in the northern part of Italy which need me to guide them to this spot.
And he said good-bye, and he went back to help other refugees interned in small communities in the northern part of-- of Italy.
Unfortunately, we found out later on the Germans caught him and put him on the wall and just shot him.
So Chichibo, Pacifici, our savior of the group, died a few days later.
To come back to our group, it was night.
The day of Kol Nidrei, one of the most holy days in-- Jewish life, and the few adults, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they came to the water, it was at nighttime.
And the smugglers told them, the Germans are not here right now.
The German frontier-- now is the time to cross.
They could only see very-- under very difficult circumstances, the two feet of [? all ?] under the water.
I must tell you that I came back to the same spot three years ago and showed it to my wife.
I wanted to see her where the children crossed.
So the adults, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mr.
Avien, ?] my mother, and they which were left, they join hands, walked across, and pulled, by joined hands, the whole group over.
And not one of the children was lost.
By the way, I would-- I forgot to mention something.
Joschko took along four British officers which were hiding in the area when they escaped out of the British camp.
And we thought having British with us, it would help us perhaps in negotiation with the Swiss.
They disappeared.
And Joschko took also an Tunisian family, a Jewish family, with very old people along.
And they made him a terrible hard time because they shouted and-- in situations where the children were quiet and were deadly afraid that the Germans or the Swiss will catch them.
They were not even cooperating with that.
But Joschko took them along.
And when they crossed the border-- when they crossed the river and they were on the other side, they found Swiss soldiers.
And in the first moment, Joschko thought that they're Germans because their uniform is green, and in the night you cannot really differentiate.
But they were actually Swiss soldiers.
And they took them, as wet and as dripping with water, and they brought them to a-- to a building.
But they didn't allow them and didn't give them the assurance of entry.
By crossing the river, all belongings the children carried were lost.
They were taken away with the water.
The beautiful part is that on the other side, Swiss people were able to-- to save the belongings.
They-- it washed up on the-- on the river.
And most of the things came back to the group and to Joschko.
And a few of the documents I showed you survived that way.
My mother got-- got almost all her things back.
But on the way of crossing, everything was lost.
A day or two later-- oh, I forgot to tell you.
The Swiss police or the-- the Swiss military forces in-- on the-- on the side of Ponte [?
Tresa ?] informed the authorities.
And they knew, because of Dr.
Lichtheim, that this group is coming, or will-- will eventually come.
And now we are here.
And all the children and the few adults which came with them were interned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother was interned in Montreux.
And it took a while that the-- till the Jewish community could organize a building and facilities for suddenly for 100 children.
They were unexpected, and there was nothing like that ever happened in Switzerland.
So they were able to get in a large building in [FRENCH], which is in-- in the vicinity of Montreux in the canton of [FRENCH] in Switzerland.
And all the children and Joschko and my mother and Mr.

Avien, ?] they are united there, and they really started the same hachshara-- the same [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] learning facilities they tried to have years before up to-- to this point, also in Switzerland.
How did you make contact with them?
I was in Busserach.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Dr.
Lichtheim sent me sometimes a few francs, six francs, five francs, into the camp and also notes.
In a [? month, ?] I got a letter from my mother.
So you can imagine what that meant to me.
Right.
She-- he knew--
--of her.
He knew about me, of course.
And when he made contact, he sent me the-- the note.
And of course, he also let Joschko know where we are.
We were four people and [?
Elise. ?] And Joschko asked him if it would be possible to unite-- to get us out from there and bring us into the same-- the same group again.
And I think it took three months till we were allowed to leave Busserach and join them in [FRENCH].
In [FRENCH] we stayed till August of 1945 when we got certificates.
From Palestine, from the-- actually from the British government, British Mandate Government of Palestine, for legal entry into-- into Palestine.
It took that long.
The war was already over, because they had to find a way to bring us from Switzerland to France to Spain and then charter a ship, a small-- small ship, which brought us to Haifa.
No.
The-- the-- the travel in 1945 was, through Switzerland and France and up to Portbou and then to Barcelona, was by train.
And that was to Europe, to-- to France just after the-- just after the war, a few months after the liberation actually.
And a lot of things were in, of course, still in terrible shape.
But we were free.
We were under different circumstances.
That is a very interesting story.
In-- in Switzerland, at that time, of course there was no Austrian government that you could require-- I was an Austrian citizen-- that you could get Austrian passports.
The German children couldn't get German passports.
And I forgot to mention, we took also in 100 Hungarian children, which came from the Kastner transport.
That is an interesting story, because an American negotiation started with the-- with the Nazi government, Nazi military, to liberate Jews out of the concentration camps in exchange for trucks.

And the-- the German government allowed 300 Jews to be liberated from German concentration camps into Switzerland in expectations that the American government will give them 100 trucks, which never materialized.
The negotiations, of course, broke off the moment the childrens and the-- the Jews crossed the Swiss-- Swiss border.
99% from Hungary.
And this transport was called the Kastner transport.
K-A-S-T-E-- T-- T-N-E-R.
Kastner.
Because the negotiation was done by a gentleman by the name of Kastner.
I cannot tell you that.
I-- I don't know.
That-- this is a-- I cannot really-- really verify.
But he-- he did this [INAUDIBLE].
Apparently, the German believed him.
The German released the children, came to our group in Nonantola-- in [FRENCH].
No. 300 Jews were released from German concentration camps that were adults, too.
But around 100 children came to us in [FRENCH].
Before we could travel-- we were close to 300.
We had to get papers.
There was no way that we could get German, Hungarian, Austrian passports.

So the Swiss government decided to give everyone from our group, after the British government in Palestine, the Mandate, gave us the-- the certificates to [? immigrate, ?] they gave us a passport, which is a Nansen pass, very unusual name.
Fridtjof Nansen was the first United Nation president.
He was a Norwegian Arctic explorer.
And he made a ruling that the displaced-- the refugees which lost their homeland, their nationality, should get an international accepted passport.
That was called the Nansen pass.
N-A-N-S-E-N, and passport is passport.
What it is is a piece of paper.

When you-- when you open it up, every government issuing it-- in my case, it was-- in our case, it was the Swiss government-- issues a statement that this and this person is an Austrian citizen or Norwegian citizen, Polish citizen, Dutch citizen, born so-and-so, and-- and all the-- [INAUDIBLE].
And it is only good-- good, valid, for one-- one-way travel.
Which means you could exit, but you could never enter.
And on one-- on one part of the paper are all your [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] documented entries.
On the other side of the paper is the British Mandate certificate stamp and the exit visas and also the entry visas.
So when we received, each one of us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Nansen pass, we also received the legal way of leaving Switzerland-- Switzerland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Swiss Jewish community paid the way from [FRENCH] to Barcelona.
In Barcelona, we were took-- we were taken over from the joint-- the Jewish organization there.
There entered a small ship by the name of an Portuguese tramp ship, you know, one of the small boats.
And that brought us to-- to Haifa.
In Haifa-- you must understand our group was-- was at that time 300.
But we collected now Jews from all over France, from all the camps in-- [INAUDIBLE].
They were brought to a-- to an-- to a point in Barcelona, a collecting point.
And when this ship going to-- to Palestine, to Haifa, was full to the capacity, I would say it was perhaps 700 people, including our group of 300.
It sailed.
That was August '95.
Ah, '45, I beg your pardon, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Palestine, we were the first legal immigrants.
Because before that day, only illegal immigrants arrived, which means people coming by night, you know, under the most difficult circumstance.
We came legally.
And we were immediately interned from the British in the camp of [?
Athlit, ?] and we had to stay there three weeks in quarantine.
But that was an medical quarantine.
That was nothing, you know, nothing else.
Unfortunately, the end of Aron Menczer was not a good one.
Aron Menczer saved-- I don't want to say me-- he saved us all.
His doing and his finding a way out, which [INAUDIBLE], also if you paid a lot of money, it was saving souls.
And Aron Menczer very easily could have left Vienna, either with me or with later people, and who could-- have-- could have saved himself.
Aron Menczer never was a person like that.
Because as long as he had one child under his supervision, he wouldn't leave that child.
It came to a day when the Germans-- when the Gestapo, eliminated the [NON-ENGLISH].

And with the last transport going to Poland and out to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, Theresienstadt, and from Theresienstadt they were sent to-- to the extermination camps, to-- He selected to go with the group, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he went with the children.
And Aron Menczer died in-- in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Today there is a monument in the only surviving Jewish temple in Vienna about Aron Menczer.
And of course, a book was written.
The children died with him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later on they found, written by children, all the number-- all the numbers I said-- all the names and birth dates and even addresses of the last group.
